lesson:
行く conjugates like any other godan verb — until you hit the て-form. Every く-verb follows the pattern: 書く→書いて, 聞く→聞いて. So you'd expect いく→いいて. Instead it's いって. This is the only く-verb that does this. The た-form is the same deal: いった instead of いいた. Everything else (いかない, いきます, いける) is perfectly regular. Just that one weird て/た.

The kanji 行 shows up everywhere. 銀行 (ぎんこう, bank), 旅行 (りょこう, travel), 行事 (ぎょうじ, event) — it carries this sense of going, moving, carrying out. When you see 行 in a compound, something is in motion.

One thing that trips people up: 行く can be read いく or ゆく. They mean the same thing. ゆく sounds more literary — you'll hear it in songs and formal writing. In conversation, people say いく.

Watch the particle: 学校に行く (go to school) marks the destination. 学校へ行く (head toward school) emphasizes direction. In speech に is far more common, but both work.

見る covers territory that English splits across three words: see, look at, and watch. テレビを見る (watch TV). 景色を見る (look at the scenery). 医者に見てもらう (have a doctor look at you). One verb, context does all the work.

The kanji 見 is worth a close look: it's 目 (eye) sitting on top of 儿 (legs). An eyeball walking around, looking at things. Once you see that image it's hard to unsee. The kanji shows up constantly: 見える (to be visible), 見せる (to show), 見つける (to find), 意見 (opinion — literally "idea" + "seeing").

Conjugation is ichidan, so it's completely predictable: chop る, add whatever you need. 見ない, 見て, 見た, 見られる. No tricks.

The most useful pattern built on this word: 〜てみる. Attach みる to another verb's て-form and it means "try doing" something to see what happens. 食べてみる (try eating it), 聞いてみる (try asking). It comes directly from the meaning of 見る — you do the action to see the result. You'll reach for this one daily.

多い is an い-adjective with a quirk that will trip you up if you don't know about it. You can say 人が多い (there are many people) — no problem. But you can't say 多い人 the way you'd say 大きい犬 (big dog). It sounds wrong. If you want to put it before a noun, it becomes 多くの人. This isn't optional — 多い strongly resists sitting directly in front of a noun.

Its opposite 少ない (すくない, few) has the exact same restriction. They're a matched pair of grammatical oddballs.

The kanji 多 is two 夕 (evening) stacked on top of each other. The old logic: many evenings have passed → many. Two of the same thing piled up means "a lot" — easy to remember.

In real use, 多い almost always appears at the end of a phrase with が marking what there's a lot of: 最近、雨が多い (there's been a lot of rain lately). 日本は祝日が多い (Japan has a lot of holidays). That's its natural position — after the thing it describes, not before it.
